title: CBA director warns financial crisis 'likely' amid rising alarm bells
full_text:
"Most people assume catastrophe is more remote than it is."
It's a passing word of warning in a new book by banker Harrison Young, who knows a thing or two about risk in the financial system. The former Commonwealth Bank director and pervious chairman of the bank's risk committee, now 82, is passing on his lifetime of lessons from a colourful personal history in some of the biggest financial institutions in the world.
It's been nearly two decades since US investment banks made billions of dollars of bets on exotic and 'synthetic' financial products tied to bundles of mortgages that weren't properly understood by ratings agencies. As the underlying loans went bad the massive losses were amplified and the cascading impact saw the financial system seize up and the crisis ripple through economies around the world.
Lessons were learned. But could we soon see another crisis in the global financial system?
"I think it's likely, but it could take a while," Young told Yahoo Finance.
The American born Harvard graduate worked as a reporter at the Washington Post before serving in the Vietnam War. He joined Wall Street's Citibank in 1971 before moving to rival Morgan Stanley. Young served as the chief executive of China's first investment banking firm before joining CBA as a non-executive director in 2007, just as the GFC was beginning to unfold.
"I remember it well," he said, recalling trying to navigate the customs of his new role at the top of Australia's banking sector while global markets crumbled.
Australia fared better than most economies during the period as banks largely kept lending. As former Treasury Secretary Ken Henry noted in a 2010 review, concerns at the time that credit would have to be rationed "did not occur to any great extent".
But complacency over quietly "compounding" risks can be a killer, Young says.
"Every so often, banks are discovered to be in trouble or to have misbehaved. There is a crisis or at least an outcry. New regulations are imposed. The supervisory architecture is redesigned. Bankers promise to do better. For a while, they do," he writes in Prudence and Ambition: How banks work and why their governance needs to change.
"As time passes since the last crisis, risk aversion leaks out of people, institutions and financial systems."
There hasn't really been a major financial crisis in global markets in the age of social media. But when it inevitably comes, Young expects it to be an unforeseen 'black swan' event.
"I think it will happen in a way that causes us to say; 'How could we have not thought about that?'," he told Yahoo Finance.
Aussie financial institutions put on notice over growing risks
Last week, the financial regulator warned it has grown increasingly concerned about the potential for adverse impacts on the financial system stemming from geopolitical shocks such as trade restrictions, sanctions and armed conflicts.
The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) said it had written to banks, insurers and superannuation funds setting out its minimum expectations in relation to their readiness for geopolitical shocks in an increasingly turbulent world.
Although most entities are aware of geopolitical risks, APRA and other agencies on the Council of Financial Regulators have observed common gaps in how the country's financial institutions respond to that reality with practical risk management and crisis preparedness, the regulator said.
"As a mid-size trade-exposed economy, Australia will always be impacted by what happens in the rest of the world – and right now the rest of the world is becoming more volatile and unpredictable," APRA Chair John Lonsdale said.
The letter was "a clear call to action as awareness is not enough," he added.
The Council of Financial Regulators (APRA, ASIC and the RBA) also held its quarterly meeting this week, noting in a statement on Tuesday "the outlook for financial stability warranted a high degree of vigilance" and that "global financial stability risks remained elevated".
Young believes "it's probably more important that banks be well managed than well regulated.
"But we'd like both," he quipped.
Australian debt to GDP has surged since GFC
After being lauded for nearly three decades without a recession, the Australian economy has struggled with inflation and continued low productivity growth in recent years. High levels of government debt (close to $1 trillion in gross debt) is matched with plenty of household debt as high home prices have pushed more Australians into levels of mortgage stress near levels last seen during the GFC, Roy Morgan analysis showed this week.
While lower than many peers, Australia's debt to GDP has surged since the GFC, with the Parliamentary Budget Office's latest National Fiscal Outlook noting the national accounts had "worsened" due to rising government expenses.
"The increase in government debt to levels not experienced in generations raises questions about the sustainability of this level of debt," a briefing book for the current parliament notes.
Meanwhile an AI-fuelled frenzy – and huge amount of liquidity in the system – have seen global share markets continue to surge higher, prompting many to wonder when the long running bull market will end.
While there's a huge difference between a correction, a crash and a crisis, when the big financial crisis does eventually come, Australia might not be in such a good place to weather the storm compared to 2008.
author: Nick Whigham
heading: Banking and Economy
page_title: Commonwealth Bank director warns another financial crisis 'likely' as memories of GFC fade
